title: Coverage of essential health services
subtitle: Coverage of essential health services is measured as an index on a scale of 0 to 100 (where higher is better). This index is based on risk-standardized death rates – which give a measure of healthcare access and quality – and whether common interventions are carried out.
caption:
Coverage of essential health services

Coverage of essential health services is measured as an index on a scale of 0 to 100 (where higher is better). This index is based on risk-standardized death rates – which give a measure of healthcare access and quality – and whether common interventions are carried out.

This choropleth world map displays 2023 coverage of essential health services, measured as an index on a scale of 0 to 100 where higher values indicate better coverage. The legend ranges from 0 to 100, with tick marks at 20-point intervals, and uses a blue color scale where lighter shades represent lower index values and darker shades represent higher values. North America, Western Europe, Australia, and parts of East Asia including Japan and South Korea appear in the darkest blue shades, indicating index values in the 80 to 100 range. Much of Latin America, including Brazil, Argentina, and Mexico, sits in mid-to-dark blue shades that fall between 60 and 80. Eastern Europe and Russia show a mix of mid-range blues. Across Africa, there is substantial variation: North African countries like Egypt and Morocco appear in lighter to mid-range blues, while many Sub-Saharan African countries including Chad, the Central African Republic, and Somalia sit in the lightest shades of the legend, suggesting index values below 40. South Asia shows India and Pakistan in lighter blue shades, while Bangladesh appears slightly darker. The Middle East displays variation, with Gulf states in darker blues and Yemen in lighter shades. Greenland and a few small island territories show hatching, indicating no data available. The healthcare index used to assess coverage is based on health quality and access indicators and risk-standardized death rates, acknowledging that countries provide services for health promotion, prevention, treatment, and care, including rehabilitation and palliation.
